verdict_reasoning: ATP5F1B canonically resides on the mitochondrial inner membrane (matrix-side peripheral protein, no TM domain of its own). However, extensive evidence documents an 'ectopic' surface pool: flow cytometry confirms ATP5B protein on the outer surface of leukemia (NB4, HL-60), endothelial, hepatocyte, adipocyte, and cancer cell lines. Surface trafficking occurs via mitochondrial membrane fusion to the plasma membrane (DRP1/KIF5B-dependent). The surface complex retains catalytic activity and binds anti-ATP5B antibodies at 4°C, indicating a stable, wash-resistant surface pool. OMIM cites its role as an ecto-ApoA-I receptor on hepatocytes. Surface expression is cell-type- and context-restricted, qualifying as contextual.
